question: Umpire: "We started 6 minutes late today, batting side reckons they should still get the full 35 overs. What do I actually give them?"
expected_answer: Deduct overs using MYCA's time-lost lookup table, not a flat 1-over-per-4-minutes formula: 6 minutes lost falls in the table's 5-8 minute band, a 2-over deduction, giving 33 overs allowed, not 34. If the bowling side then goes over its allotted time for those 33 overs, apply a separate over-rate deduction on top of the time-lost deduction.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 3.3 Late starts due to other than weather conditions (sub-clause 3.3.2) and Section 17.2 (Appendix B)
notes: Corrected from original '34 overs' per docs/submission.md Baseline Evaluation Findings; row 1 of original 14.

question: Umpire: "In U13 does the over end at 6 balls or 8 balls, and what if most of those extra balls are wides?"
expected_answer: The over ends at whichever limit is reached first, 6 legal deliveries or 8 balls bowled in total. Wides and no-balls count toward the 8-ball cap, so an over full of wides can end before 6 legal deliveries are bowled. Once the over ends this way, it still counts as one over bowled and 6 balls bowled for all other purposes in the rules.
expected_source: MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, Section J15 OVERS
notes: Row 2 of original 14, unchanged.

question: Umpire: "If the 8th ball in a junior over turns out to be a no-ball, does the batter still get a free hit, or has the over already ended?"
expected_answer: The junior playing conditions set the 8-ball cap and the no-ball/free hit rule separately, but do not state which one governs when they collide on the same delivery.
expected_source: absence
notes: Row 3 of original 14, unchanged.

question: Captain: "If it rains after 20 overs and we can't finish, how do we decide the winner?"
expected_answer: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules rule 5.3.8: if a result is not achieved after the minimum overs are completed, the match is a draw. There is no run-rate-based revised-target method in MYCA's playing conditions.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 5.3.8
notes: Corrected from a fabricated 'revised target method' per Baseline Evaluation Findings; row 4 of original 14. Section number corrected to 5.3.8 (verified against actual corpus text, not the 5.2.2 the earlier prose summary implied).

question: Parent: "My son's in the U13s, why do their overs sometimes run longer than 6 balls, is that even allowed?"
expected_answer: Yes. Junior grade overs can run up to 8 balls if there are wides or no-balls, because the over only ends once 6 legal deliveries or 8 total balls have been bowled, whichever comes first.
expected_source: MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, Section J15 OVERS
notes: Row 5 of original 14, unchanged.

question: Umpire: "Does the women's one-day comp use the same powerplay overs as the senior comp?"
expected_answer: No. No MYCA document, at any grade, mentions powerplay overs or powerplay fielding restrictions at all. This is not a rule that exists in MYCA's playing conditions -- the question's premise (that a grade-specific powerplay system exists) is false.
expected_source: absence
notes: Corrected from a fabricated grade-specific powerplay claim per Baseline Evaluation Findings; row 6 of original 14. Re-confirmed zero hits for 'powerplay' across the full corpus during this dataset build.

question: Captain: "One of our players wants to transfer in from another club mid-season so he can play finals with us, can we do that?"
expected_answer: MYCA's rules keep two things separate: a one-transfer-per-season limit on PlayHQ transfers, and a games-played threshold for finals eligibility (named and actively participated in four matches during the home-and-away season). There is no single transfer cut-off date tied to finals eligibility connecting the two -- a mid-season transfer is possible (subject to the one-per-season limit) but finals eligibility is judged separately, by games played.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 9.3 PlayHQ Transfers & Permits and Section 5.8.6 Qualifications
notes: Corrected from an invented unified transfer-cutoff rule per Baseline Evaluation Findings; row 7 of original 14. Source corrected from 'MYCA By-Laws' (no such separate document exists in the ingested corpus) to the actual sections within the Senior Men's Playing Rules document.

question: Umpire: "A captain reckons MCC Laws say an over is always 6 balls, full stop, so we should ignore the 8-ball cap for U13. Is he right?"
expected_answer: No. MYCA's own junior playing conditions set the 8-ball cap for that grade, and that local rule overrides the general MCC default of a 6-ball over.
expected_source: MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, Section J15 OVERS (overrides MCC Law default)
notes: Row 9 of original 14, unchanged.

question: Captain: "Does the follow-on rule apply if we bowl the other team out cheaply in our one-day comp?"
expected_answer: Follow-on is a multi-innings concept that does not exist in MYCA's one-day playing conditions for any grade.
expected_source: absence
notes: Row 10 of original 14, unchanged. Re-confirmed zero hits for 'follow-on' across the full corpus during this dataset build.

question: Captain: "Is it going to rain Saturday at our home ground, should we plan for a delayed start?"
expected_answer: Requires a live weather forecast for the specific ground and date, which the rule documents don't contain.
expected_source: web
notes: Row 11 of original 14, unchanged.

question: Umpire: "I heard Cricket Australia changed the concussion substitute rule this season, does that apply to our grade?"
expected_answer: Requires checking Cricket Australia's current published policy and whether MYCA has adopted it, since the association's own documents may not yet reflect a recent national rule change. This question genuinely needs both: whether MYCA has adopted a Cricket Australia policy requires checking MYCA's own rules (which do not explicitly address concussion substitutes), then Cricket Australia's current guidelines, cited by source.
expected_source: web
notes: Row 12 of original 14, unchanged. This is the 'both tools correct' case -- expected_behavior kept as web_answer but the real acceptance criterion is checking both search_rules_tool (comes back empty/silent) and web_search_tool (Cricket Australia's current policy).

question: Parent: "My daughter's 12. Can she play in the boys U13 team if our club doesn't have a girls team this year?"
expected_answer: Yes, subject to age limits. A female may play in a competition with males up to 2 years below her age (this excludes a female-only competition). To be eligible for the under-14s competition, a female must not be 16 years of age as of the Friday before Round 1.
expected_source: MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, Age restriction of players
notes: Row 13 of original 14. The expected answer itself was not wrong (this was a reasoning-miss, not a wrong-expected-answer case per Baseline Evaluation Findings), but expected_source corrected from 'MYCA By-Laws' to the actual section, and expected_answer expanded from the original vaguer wording to the specific age-allowance text confirmed in the corpus.

question: Umpire: "Ball hits the sightscreen on the full, is that a six or do I call dead ball?"
expected_answer: No MYCA-specific sightscreen rule exists. The applicable rule is the general boundary-six rule: six runs are awarded if a ball hit into a tree or other obstacle grounded inside or outside the boundary would, in the umpire's opinion, have carried over the boundary on the full. This would apply to a sightscreen as a fixed obstacle, but it is not a sightscreen-specific rule.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 4.9 Boundaries (sub-clause 4.9.12)
notes: Corrected from a fabricated sightscreen-specific rule per Baseline Evaluation Findings; row 14 of original 14. Section 4.9.12 confirmed to exist for real within the '4.9 Boundaries' chunk.

question: how many max balls can be bowled in one over in women grade?
expected_answer: A maximum of 8 balls can be bowled per over in the Senior Women's grade, including all extras (no balls and wides). This differs from Senior Men's, which has no extras-inclusive cap and uses the standard 6-legal-ball over. The last over of each innings must consist of exactly 6 legal deliveries.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1, Section 4.3.5

question: what will happen if there is 13 mintes disruption in match?
expected_answer: Ambiguous without knowing (a) which grade, and (b) whether the 13 minutes was lost before play started or during an innings. If pre-match (Senior Men's Section 3.3 or Senior Women's Section 3.3), both documents' late-start tables give a 4-over deduction for 13 minutes lost (13-16 minute band). If mid-innings, the two grades use different formulas that do not necessarily produce the same result: Senior Men's Section 5.3.2(B) rounds the lost-overs figure to the next even number and halves it before applying; Senior Women's Section 5.3.2 divides the remaining available time by 4 directly, with no even-rounding step. A single confident number would be overreaching -- the honest response should ask which grade and whether this was a pre-match or mid-innings delay, or present both branches explicitly.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 3.3 / 5.3.2; MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1, Section 3.3 / 5.3.2
notes: Genuinely ambiguous on two independent axes (grade + timing). The two candidate mid-innings formulas do not converge on the same numeric answer, so this should not be scored against one 'correct' number -- the acceptance criterion is that the system asks a clarifying question (what grade, and what stage of the match the time was lost) or honestly presents multiple branches instead of confidently picking one. User confirmed this framing on 2026-07-13: the question is deliberately left as the real, ambiguous production query (verbatim, typo preserved) rather than rewritten to remove the ambiguity, since the missing grade/stage context is exactly what the system should ask for.

question: how many max balls in U14 junior cricket
expected_answer: An over in Junior (Under 14) matches ends after 6 legal deliveries or 8 total deliveries (including no balls and wides), whichever comes first. Junior grade in this corpus IS the Under 14 competition (per the Age Restriction rule, which defines Junior eligibility entirely in terms of the under-14 age cutoff) -- there is no separate 'U14' sub-distinction to resolve beyond the Junior grade itself.
expected_source: MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, Section J15 OVERS

question: Our first innings lost 45 minutes total to two separate rain delays in a senior men's match. How many overs come off?
expected_answer: 45 minutes falls in the Appendix B table's 45-48 minute band, which is a 12-over reduction from the total match overs.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 17.2 (Appendix B)

question: We lost 200 minutes to rain in a senior women's match today - what's the overs reduction?
expected_answer: This can't be answered with a single number without knowing when in the match the 200 minutes was lost, and the answer is not a table lookup -- Appendix B (16.2) only applies to late starts (Section 3.3.2), not mid-match rain. For a mid-innings interruption, Section 5.3.2 says to divide the remaining available time between the commencement of play and the 180-minute match window by 4 minutes to get the revised overs. A 200-minute loss exceeds the entire 180-minute window on its own, meaning there is no remaining time left to convert into overs, regardless of when in the match it happened. In that case no result can be achieved within the format, and per Section 5.3.7 ('if a result is not achieved after the minimum overs are completed, the match is a draw'), the match is a draw, not a numeric overs reduction. A well-formed system answer should reason through this -- check which rule applies (late start vs. mid-match), apply the grade's own formula, and recognize when the deduction exceeds what's left to bowl -- rather than confidently stating a fixed overs number.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1, Section 5.3.2 (mid-match rain formula), Section 5.3.7 (draw rule) -- not 16.2/Appendix B, which only applies to late starts per Section 3.3.2
notes: Rewritten on 2026-07-13 after the user rejected the original ('50 overs off' via Appendix B -- impossible since Senior Women's innings total is only 20 overs). Re-verification confirmed: Appendix B governs late starts only (3.3.2); mid-match rain uses 5.3.2's own formula (divide remaining time to the 180-minute match window by 4); and per the user's own reasoning, a deduction must be checked against what's left to bowl -- if it exceeds that, the match can't produce a result and reverts to a draw (5.3.7), not a forced numeric answer. expected_behavior changed from answer to flag_acceptable since the correct response requires multi-step reasoning through the right branch (late start vs. mid-match, then a result-possible-or-not check), not a single confident number.

question: How many overs come off if a senior men's side is late to start by 10 minutes?
expected_answer: 10 minutes falls in the 9-12 minute band of the late-start table, which is a 3-over deduction from that side's over entitlement.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 3.3

question: In under 14 cricket, how many overs can one bowler bowl per day?
expected_answer: A bowler is limited to 5 overs per day in an Under 14 One-Day match, with a limit of 3 overs per spell.
expected_source: MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, Section J16: INDIVIDUAL BOWLING RESTRICTIONS

question: Senior men's match lost 60 minutes total during the first innings due to weather. How many overs and minutes come off each team's allocation?
expected_answer: 60 minutes lost = 15 overs, rounded up to the next even number = 16 overs. Divide overs and time lost by 2: 8 overs and 30 minutes come off each team's allocation, so each team receives 27 overs in 105 minutes.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 5.3.2(B) (uses the rule's own worked example verbatim)

question: If a senior men's match starts at 2pm (the maximum allowed delay) and reverts to the 20-over format, how many overs can one bowler bowl?
expected_answer: Bowlers are limited to a maximum of one fifth of the overs remaining. For a 20-over match, that is 20 / 5 = 4 overs maximum per bowler.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 5.3.4
notes: The 'one fifth of overs remaining' phrasing is a genuine arithmetic step (20/5=4) but a simple one; kept it in this category rather than table_lookup since it requires applying a formula, not looking up a row.

question: Senior women's match: the team bowling second finishes 3 overs short of the minimum required, 80 minutes from the start of the second innings. How many penalty runs does the batting team get?
expected_answer: Per Section 5.3.5, 12 penalty runs per over are awarded to the batting team when the fielding side fails to bowl the minimum required overs in the second innings. 3 overs short = 3 x 12 = 36 penalty runs.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1, Section 5.3.5
notes: Corrected on 2026-07-13: the original question ('the fielding side finished 3 overs short...') was ambiguous about which side was fielding late -- 5.3.3 and 5.3.5 impose completely different penalties depending on whether the team bowling first or the team bowling second is the one running over. Reworded to explicitly specify 'team bowling second' so it unambiguously matches the 12-runs-per-over mechanism in 5.3.5. See eval-049 for the companion scenario (team bowling first late -> overs deducted from their own batting, not runs to the opponent).

question: A club forfeits in round 8 of the senior men's season without giving notice. What's the fine?
expected_answer: Round 7-9 forfeits without notice are fined $200 per forfeit, plus the umpire fees.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 3.5.2
notes: Borderline direct_rule_lookup rather than arithmetic (it's a banded lookup, not a calculation) -- flagging for the human to re-bucket if they disagree with the category placement.

question: Junior match: we lost 21 minutes total during the day for reasons nobody was responsible for. How many overs come off the daily total?
expected_answer: The daily overs are reduced by one over for each full 3 minutes of total time lost, once total time lost exceeds 15 minutes (the rule's own worked example confirms this is a flat divide over the total, not the excess: 18 minutes lost = 6 overs reduction, i.e. 18/3, not (18-15)/3). For 21 minutes lost: 21 / 3 = 7 overs come off the daily total (60 overs in Under 14's, reduced to 53).
expected_source: MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, Section J14 DAILY OVERS - One Day Matches
notes: Re-verified this session directly against the corpus. The correct section is 'J14 DAILY OVERS - One Day Matches' (my earlier claim that no J14 section existed was wrong -- it does exist, I'd been searching the wrong heading). The document gives its own worked example (15 min lost = 0 overs, 18 min lost = 6 overs), which confirms the flat-divide reading (total minutes / 3) rather than an excess-over-15 reading. User confirmed 3 minutes per over for Junior and resolved the ambiguity on 2026-07-13 after an initial back-and-forth (a first proposed number, 16 min = 4 overs, conflicted with the document's own example and was corrected).

question: Does MYCA use the Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method for rain-affected matches?
expected_answer: No. MYCA does not mention Duckworth-Lewis-Stern or any DLS-style method anywhere in its documents. Rain-affected matches are handled through MYCA's own overs-reduction table (Appendix B) instead.
expected_source: absence

question: If both teams are still tied after a Grand Final decider, is there a Super Over to break the tie?
expected_answer: No Super Over or equivalent tie-breaker mechanism is mentioned anywhere in MYCA's documents. The rules only describe ladder-position tiebreakers for a drawn or tied semi-final or grand final (the team finishing higher on the regular-season ladder is awarded the match) and a straightforward tie result when both sides score equal runs.
expected_source: absence

question: Can an umpire reverse his decision once he's made it?
expected_answer: MYCA does not define this in its own documents -- it's a base Law of Cricket, not an association playing condition. Per MCC Law 31.4 ("Correcting a decision"): an umpire may alter a decision, but only if done promptly, before the game has moved on to the next ball or action. Once that window passes, the original decision is final.
expected_source: web (MCC Law 31.4, via Tavily -- MYCA's documents do not define this)
notes: Corrected on 2026-07-13 per user guidance: general-knowledge items should not just say 'not covered' (honest_fallback) -- per CLAUDE.md's own architecture, Tavily is explicitly tasked with base Laws of Cricket questions the association's documents assume rather than define. Template: first state the association has no playing condition on this, then answer via MCC Laws/ICC playing conditions. expected_behavior changed from honest_fallback to web_answer accordingly.

question: What counts as an LBW dismissal?
expected_answer: MYCA does not define this in its own documents (the Junior document has a section titled 'LBW Rule', but it only states whether the LBW law is in effect for junior matches and who rules on it in the absence of an official umpire -- it does not define the dismissal itself). Per MCC Law 36 (LBW): a batter is out if a fair-delivered ball, not pitching outside leg stump, would have gone on to hit the stumps but is intercepted by any part of the batter's body (other than a hand holding the bat) in line with the stumps -- or outside off stump if the batter made no genuine attempt to play the ball.
expected_source: web (MCC Law 36, via Tavily -- MYCA's documents do not define the dismissal itself)
notes: Corrected on 2026-07-13, same pattern as eval-028/030/031: expected_behavior changed from honest_fallback to web_answer (MCC Law via Tavily), per CLAUDE.md's own architecture for base Laws of Cricket questions. Still worth watching in practice: the Junior document DOES have a section literally titled 'LBW Rule', so a real risk is the system retrieving that chunk and answering from it as if it defined the dismissal -- it only covers applicability/officiating, not the technical criteria.

question: What makes a delivery a no-ball or a wide, under the basic Laws of Cricket?
expected_answer: MYCA does not define the base no-ball/wide rules in its own documents -- only its own additional restrictions on top (synthetic-pitch width no-balls, waist-height no-balls, short-pitched-delivery limits, on-side fieldsman wides). Per MCC Laws 21 and 22: a no-ball covers infractions such as overstepping the popping crease, a bent-arm throw, or the ball bouncing more than twice or rolling before reaching the batter; a wide is any delivery passing out of the batter's reach from a normal guard position.
expected_source: web (MCC Laws 21 and 22, via Tavily -- MYCA's documents only define their own modifications on top of these)
notes: Corrected on 2026-07-13, same pattern as eval-028/029/031: expected_behavior changed from honest_fallback to web_answer (MCC Laws via Tavily), per CLAUDE.md's own architecture for base Laws of Cricket questions.

question: How does an umpire signal a short run?
expected_answer: MYCA does not define umpire signals in its own documents. Per the MCC Laws' standard signal set: the umpire bends one arm and touches the near shoulder with the fingers of that hand.
expected_source: web (MCC Laws' standard umpire signals, via Tavily -- MYCA's documents do not describe umpire signals)
notes: Corrected on 2026-07-13, same pattern as eval-028/029/030: expected_behavior changed from honest_fallback to web_answer (MCC Laws via Tavily), per CLAUDE.md's own architecture for base Laws of Cricket questions.

question: What was the result of last Saturday's Round 5 match between two of our division's clubs?
expected_answer: I don't know -- I only have MYCA's playing conditions and rules, not match results or fixtures. Check PlayHQ for the actual result.
expected_source: none -- out of scope entirely, not a web-searchable question
notes: Corrected on 2026-07-13 per user: this is not a case for Tavily/web_answer -- a specific private club match result isn't something a general web search would reliably surface either, so the correct behavior is a plain honest fallback ('I only know MYCA's playing conditions/rules, not results'), not an attempt to answer via web search. expected_behavior changed from web_answer to honest_fallback.

question: What time do matches start on round 1?
expected_answer: Ambiguous without knowing which grade -- start times differ: Senior Men's commences at 12pm; Senior Women's commences in one of three Saturday windows starting 8:30am/10am/1:30pm; Junior (Under 14) matches commence at 11am (12:30pm for West Division). This is MYCA's own round-1 scheduling per its rules, not a query about some other real-world tournament's 'Round 1' that would need a web search -- but the correct system response should ask which grade rather than guess or list all three unprompted.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2 Section 5.3.1; MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1 Section 5.3.1; MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1 Section J13/Hours of Play
notes: Corrected on 2026-07-13 per user: no grade is specified, so the correct behavior is to ask which grade, not answer for all three unprompted. expected_behavior changed from answer to flag_acceptable. Still true that this is the exact phrasing pattern that misfired to web_search_tool during earlier live testing (misread 'round 1' as a real-world tournament reference) -- the fix for that failure mode is answering from MYCA's own rules rather than searching the web, but the grade ambiguity is a separate, additional issue.

question: How many overs is an innings?
expected_answer: This differs by grade and isn't specified in the question: Senior Men's innings are a maximum of 35 overs; Senior Women's innings are a maximum of 20 overs; Junior (Under 14) innings are a maximum of 30 overs per side (60 total). The honest answer either asks which grade or states all three rather than silently assuming one.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2 Section 5.3.3; MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1 Section 5.3.3; MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1 Section J14
notes: Acceptance criterion here is either a grade-clarifying question or an honest all-three-grades answer -- silently picking one grade's number (most likely Senior Men's, the historically 'default' grade) should count as a miss.

question: Is the ball we're using approved?
expected_answer: Ambiguous on which grade -- MYCA specifies a different approved ball per grade (e.g. Senior Women's specifies a 142gm pink Kookaburra, differing from Senior Men's and Junior's own specifications), so the correct response should ask which grade rather than assume one. There's a second, separate ambiguity too: 'approved' could mean MYCA's own approved-ball rule for that grade, or the base Laws-of-Cricket ball specification, which MYCA's documents don't define -- the two readings route to different answers.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2 Section 4.10 / MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1 Section 4.10 (for the rules reading, grade-dependent); general-knowledge (for the Laws-of-Cricket reading)
notes: Confirmed on 2026-07-13 per user: primary ambiguity is grade (different balls per grade), not just the MYCA-rule-vs-Laws-of-Cricket reading I'd originally emphasized. Correct behavior asks which grade rather than assuming one, or presents a clean per-grade breakdown.

question: Is there a game on today?
expected_answer: I don't have a schedule -- I only know MYCA's playing conditions and rules, not fixtures. Check the MYCA website or PlayCricket for today's schedule.
expected_source: none -- out of scope entirely, not a rules question

Recategorized from ambiguous_phrasing to web_routing and expected_behavior changed from flag_acceptable to honest_fallback.

question: What's the forfeit fine for round 3, with proper notice given?
expected_answer: Differs by grade: Senior Men's and Junior both charge $100 for a round 1-6 forfeit with notice (round 3 falls in that band). Senior Women's charges a flat $50 for any round 1-7 forfeit with notice, regardless of round number.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2 Section 3.5.2; MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1 Section 25.2.2; MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1 Section 3.5.2

question: What's the maximum number of overs one bowler can bowl in an innings?
expected_answer: Differs by grade: Senior Men's caps a bowler at 7 overs (out of a 35-over innings); Senior Women's caps at 4 overs (out of a 20-over innings); Junior caps bowlers at 3 overs per spell and 5 overs per day (out of 30 overs per side).
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2 Section 5.5.5; MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1 Section 5.5.4; MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1 Section J16

question: At what score does a batter have to retire?
expected_answer: Differs by grade and is structurally different for Junior: Senior Men's retire not-out at 50 (100 in Premier Grade specifically); Senior Women's retire not-out at 50 flat (no Premier Grade exception exists in the Women's competition); Junior uses a two-stage system -- compulsory retirement at 30 runs, with the player eligible to return once all others have made 30 or been dismissed, then a further compulsory retirement at 50 if they return.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2 Section 5.5.6/6.6; MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1 Section 5.5.5; MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, 'Deliveries off the Pitch'/'If a batsman breaches the limit' sections

question: How many players do we need to name before the toss?
expected_answer: Differs by grade: Senior Men's allow naming up to 12 players (11 bat, only 11 field at once, all 12 can bowl); Senior Women's allow naming up to 11 players (only 9 can field or bowl at once, innings ends at the fall of the 8th wicket); Junior allow up to 13 players named (max 11 field at once). All three require a minimum of 7 players present to start.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2 Section 5.1; MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1 Section 5.1; MYCA Junior Playing Rules v1, 'J2 NUMBER OF PLAYERS IN TEAMS'

question: What's the deadline for emailing a player conduct report after the match?
expected_answer: All conduct reports must be emailed to the MYCA Secretary within 24 hours of the match concluding, and cc'd to the Umpires' Coordinator. The MYCA Secretary must also be notified of the report by phone/SMS on the day of the offence.
expected_source: MYCA Player Conduct Report v1
notes: Fills a gap: no golden-set item previously covered either operational form. Grade-agnostic, as the form itself is not grade-specific.

question: One of our bowlers has a suspect action, what's the process for raising it?
expected_answer: The umpire(s) must first raise it with the MYCA Association Secretary and Umpires' Coach using the Suspect Bowling Action Form only -- not in open discussion. The Secretary then contacts the player's club to investigate and take remedial action if necessary. The process is strictly confidential: umpires must not discuss or publicise concerns about a bowler's action, and a separate form is required per player. If a team or individual raises a concern, the umpire must inform the captain that MYCA's Suspect Bowling Policy protocol applies and the bowler continues bowling with no further discussion; continued complaints result in a conduct report instead. The form must be emailed to the MYCA Secretary (cc Umpires' Coordinator) within 48 hours of the match concluding.
expected_source: MYCA Suspect Bowling Action Form v1
notes: Fills a gap: no golden-set item previously covered either operational form. Grade-agnostic, as the form itself is not grade-specific.

question: At what score does a batter retire not out in senior women's grade?
expected_answer: Upon reaching 50 runs, a batter shall retire not out. There is no Premier Grade exception in Senior Women's (unlike Senior Men's, where Premier Grade specifically raises this to 100). Once all batsmen are retired or dismissed, retired batsmen may resume their innings in order of retirement.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1, Section 5.5.5
notes: Added to help balance grade_scope coverage -- Senior Women's was underrepresented (3 items) relative to Senior Men's (13) and Junior (8) in the first assembly pass.

question: What's the forfeit fine in senior women's grade if notice is given?
expected_answer: A flat $50 fine applies for any forfeit with notice, for any round 1 through 7, regardless of round number. This differs from Senior Men's, which tiers the fine by round ($100 for rounds 1-6, $200 for rounds 7-9).
expected_source: MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1, Section 3.5.2
notes: Added to help balance grade_scope coverage -- Senior Women's was underrepresented (3 items) relative to Senior Men's (13) and Junior (8) in the first assembly pass.

question: What is the MYCA Code of Conduct say about players and clubs?
expected_answer: The MYCA Code of Conduct states that all players, clubs, club officials, or spectators having an affinity with a club shall be bound by the Laws of Cricket, the spirit of cricket, and will be subject to the Association's rules. Team captains are expected to carry out their responsibilities for the proper conduct of matches within the spirit of the game and in accordance with the MCC Laws of Cricket as defined in Law 42 'Player's Conduct'.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 10 MYCA Code of Conduct / Section 11.1
notes: Query phrasing is intentionally poor-grammar (Ragas' query_style=POOR_GRAMMAR persona simulation, not a generation defect). Code of Conduct content may be grade-agnostic in practice even though sourced from the Senior Men's document -- worth checking whether Junior/Senior Women's have their own near-identical Code of Conduct sections.

question: What happen if a club wanna report a player or someone else to the MYCA and what they need to do?
expected_answer: If a club wants to report a player, another club, a club official, or a spectator connected with the club, they must lodge a written report on the alleged incident to the Association Secretary or grade secretary within 15 days of the match conclusion. This report must be endorsed by both the club President and Secretary (naming them on an electronically lodged report is sufficient), and they must lodge a bond of $200, which may be fully, partially, or not refunded based on the Executive Committee's recommendation. If the report arises from a match, it must also be endorsed by the captain of the concerned team.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 11.4 Reporting by clubs
notes: Ragas originally generated this as 'MCA' not 'MYCA' (a persona-simulated typo/abbreviation, poor-grammar query style). Corrected to 'MYCA' per user's verification pass on 2026-07-13, since the club-reporting rule itself is not in question -- only the wording.

question: What does the Executive Committee do?
expected_answer: This question as generated is broader than its source grounding: the reference material only supports a narrow answer (the Executive Committee determines further action, including suspension, for a player who fails to appear at a representative match they'd agreed to play). The Executive Committee's broader role (finals eligibility, fines, discipline, registrations, etc.) appears throughout many other sections of the document.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 12 Inter-association / representative matches
notes: WEAK ITEM -- the question as generated ('What does the Executive Committee do?') is much broader than the single narrow chunk (Section 12) the synthesizer grounded it in. question: What happens if a player does not comply with the rules regarding umpire injuries?
expected_answer: If a player fails to comply with the rules regarding umpire injuries, the matter shall be reported to the Assistant/Secretary Manager by the umpire(s), or if no official umpire, by the opposing club, within 48 hours following the completion of the match. Such reports will result in the player appearing before the Disciplinary Committee.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 15.4/15.5

question: Senior women's match: the team bowling first takes 12 minutes longer than allowed to complete their overs, past the 80-minute mark. What's the penalty?
expected_answer: Per Section 5.3.3, if the team bowling first does not complete their stipulated overs by 80 minutes since the start of the innings, overs are deducted from their own batting innings at a rate of one over for every 4 minutes over. 12 minutes late = 12 / 4 = 3 overs deducted from their own batting innings (not runs awarded to the opponent).
expected_source: MYCA Senior Women's Playing Rules v1, Section 5.3.3
notes: Added on 2026-07-13 as the companion scenario to eval-023's correction: the team-bowling-first case uses a completely different penalty mechanism (self-inflicted overs deducted from their own batting innings) than the team-bowling-second case (runs awarded to the opponent, eval-023/5.3.5). Both needed testing since they're easy to conflate.

question: Senior men's match: team batting first scores 177 for 8 in their full 35 overs. Rain interrupts the second innings after 5 overs, causing a 31-minute delay, and the second team's overs are reduced to 27. They finish on 145 for 7 in 27 overs, short of the 178 target. Who wins?
expected_answer: Nobody -- it's a draw. The target stays at 178; MYCA does not use Duckworth-Lewis-Stern or any run-rate revision method (see eval-026), only the overs are reduced, not the target. The team bowling first can only win by taking all 10 wickets -- restricting the chasing team's run rate alone isn't enough to win. Since the second team finished 145/7 (3 wickets in hand) and didn't reach 178, no result was achieved within the (reduced) overs, so per Section 5.3.8 the match is a draw.
expected_source: MYCA Senior Men's Playing Rules v2, Section 5.3.8 (draw rule) and Section 5.3.2 / Appendix B (overs reduction), read together with eval-026's DLS-absence finding
notes: Added on 2026-07-13 at the user's request, following up on their eval-026 verification. Tests a genuinely tricky compound scenario: rain interrupts only the second innings, overs get reduced but the target does NOT get revised (no DLS-style method exists), and the team bowling first can only win by taking all 10 wickets, not by merely restricting the run rate -- a system that answers 'first team wins' by comparing final scores alone would be wrong. Grounded in Section 5.3.8's draw rule, already used and verified via eval-004.
